facade_colour: weathered red brick with pale yellow brick banding and white trim

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair of semi-detached houses, the properties retain most of their original building features including mirrored facades, cross-gabled roofline, double-height bays, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork detailing, decorative wooden gable brackets, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: weathered red brick with pale yellow brick banding and white trim
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: original brick body survives but windows and trim are modern replacements; facade shows patching and repainting at openings
photo_observations.window_details: flat-headed paired upper windows with simple sills; lower facade partly obscured by neighbouring pair composition
photo_observations.door_details: single residential door with white trim
photo_observations.cornice_details: small projecting roof edge under front gable
photo_observations.porch_details: short raised stoop behind low railing
photo_observations.alterations_visible: replacement windows and simplified porch/entry details
photo_observations.notes: Side/rear view showing red brick Victorian house and adjacent BURDOCK rooftop signage (red letters). Graffiti-covered white-painted garage/outbuilding wall visible. Chain-link fence with dormant vines. The actual 12 Denison facade is partially visible: red brick, 2 windows on upper floor. Side view only, limited facade visibility.

facade_description: 12 Denison Sq presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include string courses, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair of semi-detached houses, the properties retain most of their original building features including mirrored facades, cross-gabled roofline, double-height bays, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork detailing, decorative wooden gable brackets, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
